sentences.base_language: One morning, Carlos woke up with a headache and a stomachache, and felt very tired.
sentences.target_language: Una mañana, Carlos se despertó con dolor de cabeza y de estómago, y se sentía muy cansado.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Dolor de** + body part = pain in ~: dolor **de** cabeza (headache), dolor **de** estómago (stomachache), dolor **de** espalda (backache). No article between **de** and the body part in this fixed structure.
**Se sentía** — imperfect of sentirse: the continuous background feeling of tiredness. Compare: **se sintió** cansado (felt tired — a single moment, preterite). Here the imperfect conveys that the tiredness was an ongoing state he woke into, not a discrete event.

sentences.base_language: Lately he had been working a lot and wasn't sleeping well.
sentences.target_language: Últimamente había estado trabajando mucho y no dormía bien.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Había estado trabajando** — pluperfect progressive: **había** (imperfect of haber) + **estado** + gerund. This tense expresses an ongoing action that was happening in the past before another past reference point. Compare: **había trabajado** (had worked — pluperfect, completed) vs **había estado trabajando** (had been working — ongoing process).
**Últimamente** = lately/recently — a very useful adverb. **No dormía bien** uses the imperfect for a habitual/ongoing past state: he wasn't sleeping well (as a pattern, not one night).

sentences.base_language: He decided to go to the hospital near his home.
sentences.target_language: Decidió ir al hospital que estaba cerca de su casa.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Decidir** + infinitive = to decide to do: decidió **ir** (decided to go). No preposition between **decidir** and the infinitive — unlike **decidirse a** (to make up one's mind to), which adds **a**: se decidió **a** ir (finally made up his mind to go).
**El hospital que estaba cerca** — relative clause with the imperfect: **estaba** describes the hospital's location as a background fact. The relative pronoun **que** (that/which) refers back to **el hospital**. The imperfect here is used because the location is a descriptor, not an event.

sentences.base_language: He washed his face and got dressed, but felt too weak to eat breakfast.
sentences.target_language: Se lavó la cara y se vistió, pero se sentía demasiado débil para desayunar.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se lavó la cara** — reflexive + body part with definite article: se lavó **la** cara (not *su* cara). With reflexive verbs and body parts, Spanish uses the definite article to indicate possession: me duele **la** cabeza, se rompió **el** brazo.
**Demasiado + adjective + para + infinitive** = too ~ to do: demasiado **débil** para **desayunar** (too weak to eat breakfast). This structure is parallel to English 'too...to': demasiado **cansado** para **trabajar** (too tired to work), demasiado **joven** para **votar** (too young to vote).

sentences.base_language: The hospital was two subway stops from his apartment.
sentences.target_language: El hospital estaba a dos paradas de metro de su apartamento.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Estar a + distance** = to be (a certain distance) away: está **a dos paradas** (two stops away), está **a cinco minutos** (five minutes away), está **a tres kilómetros** (three kilometers away). The preposition **a** expresses this measured distance.
**Parada** = stop (for public transport): parada de **metro** (subway stop), parada de **autobús** (bus stop). The word **parada** comes from **parar** (to stop). Note the structure: **de metro** specifies what kind of stop — no article needed: paradas de metro (not *del metro*).

sentences.base_language: At the reception, a nurse asked him: "How can I help you?"
sentences.target_language: En la recepción, una enfermera le preguntó: «¿En qué le puedo ayudar?»
sentences.grammarNote:
**Le preguntó** — indirect object **le** (to him): preguntar takes an indirect object for the person asked: le pregunté (I asked him/her), le preguntaron (they asked him). Compare: **le** pedí (I asked/requested him/her — pedir also uses indirect object).
**¿En qué le puedo ayudar?** = How can I help you? — a polished service expression using the formal **le** (usted register). Informal version: ¿En qué **te** puedo ayudar? The **usted** form is used with **le** as indirect object, while the conjugated verb uses the 3rd person: le **puedo** ayudar.

sentences.base_language: He replied: "My head and stomach hurt, and I feel completely exhausted."
sentences.target_language: Él respondió: «Me duelen la cabeza y el estómago, y me siento completamente agotado.»
sentences.grammarNote:
**Me duelen** — **doler** works like **gustar**: the body part is the grammatical subject, the person is the indirect object. With two body parts (cabeza y estómago = plural), the verb is plural: me **duelen** (not *me duele*). One body part: me **duele** la cabeza. Compare: me **duele** el brazo but me **duelen** los brazos.
**Completamente agotado** = completely exhausted — **agotado** (past participle used as adjective) from **agotar** (to exhaust/drain). Note: **agotado** can also mean 'sold out' for products: el libro está **agotado** (the book is sold out).

sentences.base_language: A doctor examined him and asked: "Do you exercise regularly? How is your diet?"
sentences.target_language: Un médico lo examinó y le preguntó: «¿Hace ejercicio regularmente? ¿Cómo es su dieta?»
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lo examinó** — direct object **lo** (him): the doctor examined **him** directly → **lo**. Contrast with indirect object **le** (to him): le **preguntó** (asked **to** him). A classic Spanish distinction: direct objects (the thing affected) use **lo/la/los/las**; indirect objects (the recipient/beneficiary) use **le/les**.
**¿Cómo es su dieta?** — **ser** for habitual characteristics and identity: ¿cómo **es** tu dieta? (what is your diet like? — a description of its nature). Compare: ¿cómo **está** la sopa? (how is the soup? — its current state/taste).

sentences.base_language: He admitted he had been eating a lot of junk food and barely resting.
sentences.target_language: Admitió que había estado comiendo mucha comida basura y descansando muy poco.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Admitir que** + past tense — reporting past facts: admitió que **había estado comiendo** (admitted he had been eating). The pluperfect progressive (**había estado** + gerund) establishes that this eating was an ongoing process before the moment of admission.
**Comida basura** = junk food, lit. 'rubbish food' — a compound noun: **comida** (food) + **basura** (rubbish/trash). Spanish compound nouns often use noun + noun without a preposition: agua **mineral** (mineral water), coche **bomba** (car bomb), hombre **rana** (frogman). **Muy poco** = very little: **poco** as an adverb after a verb — descansando **muy poco**.

sentences.base_language: The doctor said: "You need more rest and a better diet. Drink plenty of water."
sentences.target_language: El médico dijo: «Necesita más descanso y una mejor dieta. Beba mucha agua.»
sentences.grammarNote:
**Necesita** — formal usted address (3rd person singular): the doctor uses the **usted** form throughout. **Necesitar** + noun (direct object): necesitar **más descanso** (need more rest). Compare: **necesitar** + infinitive: necesitar **dormir** más (need to sleep more).
**Beba** — formal imperative of **beber** (usted form): the usted imperative comes from the present subjunctive. Regular -er verb: beb**a** (drink — usted); compare informal tú: beb**e** (drink). Other usted imperatives: **coma** (eat), **descanse** (rest), **venga** (come). These are essential for giving instructions formally.

sentences.base_language: He felt a little embarrassed, but also relieved to know it wasn't serious.
sentences.target_language: Se sintió un poco avergonzado, pero también aliviado al saber que no era nada grave.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Al saber** = upon knowing/on learning: **al** + infinitive expresses a simultaneous or immediately consequent action: **al saber** (upon learning), **al salir** (upon leaving). This is one of the most natural and expressive constructions in Spanish.
**No era nada grave** = wasn't anything serious — the double negative **no...nada** is standard in Spanish (not a grammar error): no es **nada** (it's nothing), no hay **nada** grave (there's nothing serious). **Grave** = serious/severe (for illness/injury); also means 'grave' or 'low-pitched' (for tone). **Aliviado** = relieved — from **aliviar** (to relieve/alleviate).

sentences.base_language: He left the hospital and found a grocery store nearby.
sentences.target_language: Salió del hospital y encontró un supermercado cerca.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Salió del** hospital — **salir de** = to leave/exit: salir **de** casa (leave home), salir **del** trabajo (leave work). **Del** = de + el (required contraction). Compare: **irse** (to go away, more abruptly): se fue **del** hospital.
**Encontró** — preterite of **encontrar** (stem-changing o→ue in present, but regular in preterite): encontr**ó** (found). Note: **encontrar** = to find (search complete); **buscar** = to look for (search ongoing). He found it → **encontró**; he was looking for it → **buscaba**.

sentences.base_language: He bought vegetables, fish, and fruit — no snacks this time.
sentences.target_language: Compró verduras, pescado y fruta — nada de aperitivos esta vez.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Nada de + noun** = no/none of: **nada de** aperitivos (no snacks at all), nada de azúcar (no sugar), nada de alcohol (no alcohol). This construction has a dismissive or emphatic tone — more forceful than simply **sin** aperitivos.
**Esta vez** = this time — **vez** (time/occasion) appears in many common expressions: **a veces** (sometimes), **otra vez** (again), **de vez en cuando** (from time to time), **a la vez** (at the same time), **en vez de** (instead of). Knowing these **vez** expressions greatly expands natural Spanish.

sentences.base_language: Back home, he made a simple soup with vegetables and chicken.
sentences.target_language: En casa, preparó una sopa sencilla con verduras y pollo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**En casa** = at home — no article: **en** casa (at home, not *en la casa*). Compare: **en el hospital** (at the hospital — needs the article for specific places). A handful of place expressions drop the article in Spanish: **en casa**, **en clase** (in class), **en cama** (in bed).
**Sencilla** = simple/plain/straightforward — **sencillo/a** implies ease and lack of complexity (positive connotation). Compare **simple** (which in Spanish can sound negative, like 'simplistic') and **fácil** (easy). **Sencillo** is perfect for 'a simple meal': una comida **sencilla**, una receta **sencilla**.

sentences.base_language: While cooking, he called his friend and told her about the doctor's visit.
sentences.target_language: Mientras cocinaba, llamó a su amiga y le contó sobre la visita al médico.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Mientras + imperfect** = while (background action): **mientras cocinaba** (while he was cooking — imperfect, ongoing backdrop). The main events (llamó, contó) are in the preterite. This contrast — imperfect for background vs preterite for foreground events — is one of the most important distinctions in Spanish narrative.
**Le contó sobre** = told her about — **contar** (to tell/recount) takes an indirect object: le **conté** (I told him/her), **contarle** algo a alguien (tell someone something). **Sobre** = about/on: hablar **sobre** el tema, una pregunta **sobre** historia.

sentences.base_language: His friend laughed and said: "I always told you to take better care of yourself!"
sentences.target_language: Su amiga se rió y dijo: «¡Siempre te digo que te cuides mejor!»
sentences.grammarNote:
**Siempre te digo** — present tense for habitual action: she says this to him all the time (not just once). The preterite would imply a single past event: **te dije** (I told you — once, in the past).
**Que te cuides** — subjunctive after verbs of telling/wanting: decir **que** + subjunctive when expressing a command or wish. Compare: te **digo que** estás cansado (I'm telling you that you're tired — indicative, a fact) vs te **digo que** te cuides (I'm telling you to take care — subjunctive, a directive). **Cuidarse** = to take care of oneself — a key reflexive verb.

sentences.base_language: After eating, he felt much better — his energy was returning.
sentences.target_language: Después de comer, se sintió mucho mejor — su energía estaba volviendo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Después de + infinitive** = after doing: **después de** comer (after eating). This infinitive construction requires the same subject for both clauses — you cannot use it when the subjects differ. For different subjects, use **después de que** + subjunctive: después de que **él** comiera (after he ate — if the subjects differ).
**Estaba volviendo** — past progressive (imperfect of estar + gerund): su energía **estaba volviendo** (his energy was returning — a gradual, ongoing process). Compare: su energía **volvió** (his energy returned — a single completed event, preterite). The progressive highlights the ongoing, gradual nature of the recovery.

sentences.base_language: He lay down and rested for a couple of hours.
sentences.target_language: Se acostó y descansó un par de horas.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Acostarse** = to lie down / go to bed — reflexive verb: yo me **acuesto** (I lie down), él se **acostó** (he lay down). This is distinct from **tumbar** (to knock down) or **echarse** (to lie down, more casual). **Acostarse** is the standard verb for going to bed: ¿a qué hora te acuestas? (what time do you go to bed?).
**Un par de** = a couple of / a pair of: **un par de** horas (a couple of hours), **un par de** días (a couple of days). **Par** (pair/couple) is versatile: un par de zapatos (a pair of shoes), un par de amigos (a couple of friends). More precise than **unos/unas** (some) — **un par** implies approximately two.

sentences.base_language: By evening, his mood had completely improved and he felt calm and content.
sentences.target_language: Al anochecer, su humor había mejorado por completo y se sentía tranquilo y contento.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Al anochecer** = at nightfall/by evening — **al** + infinitive used as a time expression: **al amanecer** (at dawn), **al anochecer** (at dusk/nightfall), **al llegar** (upon arriving). **Anochecer** as a noun means 'nightfall': **al** (at the moment of) **anochecer** (nightfall).
**Humor** = mood (false friend!) — in Spanish, **humor** primarily means 'mood': **buen humor** (good mood), **mal humor** (bad mood). 'Humour' (comedic sense) is also **humor**, but the mood meaning dominates in everyday usage. **Por completo** = completely: sinónimo de **completamente**, but more emphatic: cambió **por completo** (changed completely).

sentences.base_language: He promised himself to take better care of his body from now on.
sentences.target_language: Se prometió a sí mismo cuidar mejor su cuerpo de ahora en adelante.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se prometió a sí mismo** = promised himself — **sí mismo** is the emphatic reflexive pronoun for 3rd person: por **sí mismo** (by himself), para **sí misma** (for herself), confiaba en **sí mismo** (trusted himself). Adding **mismo** is optional for emphasis: se lo prometió (he promised it to himself) vs se prometió **a sí mismo** (he promised himself — emphasising self-directed action).
**De ahora en adelante** = from now on — a fixed expression: **de ahora en adelante** voy a comer mejor (from now on I'll eat better). Compare **a partir de ahora** (starting now, from this moment). Both are very natural; **de ahora en adelante** has a slightly more solemn, resolving tone.
